So kia ora koutou. I'm Phoebe Eden-Mann.
I'm the national policy analyst for CCS Disability Action.
Thank you for the opportunity to speak to you guys about this today.
We do support the need for a strong legislative foundation for the disability
support services. However, we are deeply concerned that this bill in its
current form will cause avoidable and measurable
harm to disabled people and their whanau.
Our primary recommendation is that the Select Committee require the government to
rewrite the bill in full, following meaningful consultation with disabled
people, families, whanau, and representative
organizations. Our first concern is the lack of
consultation. The bill
got introduced with very limited to no
meaningful consultation,
despite the fact that it
directly affects disabled people's lives, independence, supports, and rights.
This falls short of our obligations under the United Nations Convention on the
Rights of Persons with Disabilities, particularly the requirement to closely
consult with and actively involve disabled people
in matters that affect them. The consultation period's also been too short,
particularly of a bill of this scale and for communities that rely on
accessible information and take some time to absorb it
and process it, and then provide their views.
Our second concern is Clause 8 for the proposed families first
principle. The bill places responsibility onto families and
whanau and other culturally recognized family groups before public support is
provided. This also defines family
extremely broadly, extending well beyond immediate
family. We believe that this is unreasonable.
Disabled people should not have to exhaust their family tree before receiving
basic disability support.
Family and whanau support is really important and it's deeply valued, but it cannot
replace publicly funded support.
Without clear limits, this bill risks making families responsible for lifelong
care, regardless of their capacity, well-being, health, or financial
situation. That's not sustainable, and it increases the
risk of isolation, poverty, burnout, and unsafe care
arrangements. It also undermines disabled people's rights to live
independently and to be included in the community,
which they're guaranteed under Article 19 of the rights
of the convention.
We're especially concerned about the impact on women, older carers, and families
who are already under pressure. Evidence cited in our submission shows
that most family and whanau carers are women. Many are older.
Many experience physical and financial relationship strain because of caring.
Disabled people and their families are also more likely to experience poverty.
Asking already stretched families to provide
more before publicly available support is available is both
unrealistic and harmful.
So in summary, CCS Disability Action recommends that
the bill be rewritten after meaningful consultation with disabled people and their
whanau. If the bill proceeds, we recommend drastically
narrowing the definition of family responsibility, setting clear
limits of what families are reasonably expected to provide,
requiring assessments for carer wellbeing and sustainability
to provide,
removing provisions to allow potential means testing, and
ensure any future disability support programs
uphold the rights of disabled people.
This bill affects the independence, dignity and safety and wellbeing of
disabled people across Aotearoa.
Nothing about us without us only works if the voices of disabled people are
present, and this bill has not only failed to seek out those
voices but will have negative impact for thousands
of disabled people and their loved ones.
We urge the committee to ensure that disabled people's voices are
central and that any future law strengthens rather than weakens their
rights and supports. Thank you, and I'm happy to take any questions if you have
any.
All right. Thank you.

Thank you. My name is Lisa Martin. I'm the director of Complex Care
Group Trust, and I'm pleased to have the opportunity to share the insights and the
expertise of our organization with the Select Committee for the Disability
Support Services Bill. Complex Care Group is a disability
information advisory service predominantly assisting family
members of those with very high and complex disability needs.
And this is largely due to the nature of complex disabilities, where the
disabled person often does not have the ability to
communicate their own preferences, and for this, they depend
instead on those who deeply know and understand them, which is usually
close family.
Our main concerns about the Disability Support Services Bill,
family to hold responsibility for the care of the disabled person
in the first instance.
Families already turn to family first.
From infancy through childhood, it is typical to call upon family
first to take up kind offers of support from family and friends.
This becomes exponentially more difficult as the profoundly disabled
child grows older. To insist on family first
responsibility could put families at risk from physical injuries
and psychological trauma, especially if it involves extended
family who do not regularly provide such support.
The type of care required often involves lifting,
transferring, use of hoists, tube feeding,
oxygen use, et cetera.
Care of those with high and complex needs represents significant time,
patience, and expertise in exactly which supports are required
when and how, and why. Extended family
will likely lack training and expertise in the correct methods of providing care
and behavior management, et cetera.
Families are already exhausted. We know of some families who literally
provide support around the clock, and whilst there may be support workers
involved for some care needs, parents still contribute
extraordinarily high levels of support.
It is already family who fills the gap when support workers are unwell or
taking leave.
Risk of abuse, either from exhausted parents to the disabled
person or the disabled person to the family member.
The loss of choice and control around who can be a disabled person's support
worker could lead to immense frustration and distress for the
disabled person. Child to parent violence and abuse is a
very real thing.
Families are often dispersed across the motu or the globe.
There is no guidance in the bill as to how stringently family first
will be imposed. Family first does not seem practical in
2026 New Zealand when family members will have their own
careers, families, and commitments of their own.
The remarkably detailed list of what comprises family is
worrying. There is no guidance in the bill regarding which
circumstances might require not only parents, but siblings,
aunts, uncles, and even first cousins to
become caregivers. Requiring family first
involvement does risk fracturing family relationships.
Means testing in the same way the detailed list of family could be
interpreted as meaning that extended family members may be
means or asset tested for their disabled
family member.
Means testing preferably needs to be removed from the bill or
significantly clarified. It is said that means testing
already exists only for two supports.
Equipment and modification services and household management
requires a community services card, and means or
income testing is certainly not the only eligibility requirement for
those supports.
Means testing or income testing is likely to produce unintended
consequences. Money is likely to be spent or protected in a
trust to avoid scrutiny. And how can it be
equitable for a family who has assets that are not protected
in a trust to be required to pay?
The intended elimination of paid family carers, who are
commonly parents, is problematic.
The choice to become a paid family carer is not always a choice.
It's often driven by the lack of suitably trained or experienced staff.
Constant staff turnover drives many parents to provide the care
themselves to ensure continuity and less stress for the
disabled person. Removing this option for carers to be
paid will require them to turn to other paid work, and
a greater number of support workers will be required.
Where will they come from? Exactly who provides support should
be a choice. Paid family members, or family being
employed by a provider organization, or the employment of external
staff. These are the choices that need to be retained so disabled
people and families can select the best option for them.
We request the bill be abolished and a fresh approach involving
consultation prior to the writing of a disability support services
bill is undertaken. Family carers have huge
wisdom to share. Thank you.
Thank you very much, Lisa. Very good submission, and I note your
recommendations in the back of your submission, which we will take note of.
Sadly, we've run out of time for questions, but
we will definitely look at your recommendations, which were very clear. Thank you.
Thank you.
Thanks for your time.
Thank you.

I am Rebecca Graham, and I am the chair of the Parent/Family/Whānau
Network. The network is a collective of nationally relevant grassroot
organizations that variously represent the shared lived experience of
parents and whānau within the disability sector.
We have extensive lived experience and sector knowledge of the realities of
caring and being family within a disability context.
Network members would have been more than happy to share our
expertise and knowledge with the minister in developing this bill.
We were never asked.
We did not even know about this bill until it was before Parliament.
Disabled people have generously expended their time, energy, and
resource over this week to share their knowledge and expertise.
I'm immensely proud to be part of such a generous, caring, and diverse sector.
Diversity means we often disagree,
yet this bill has united us. Disabled people, families,
advocates, carers, organizations,
services, and providers have all found this bill to be deeply
flawed. When the whole sector is united,
it is sensible to listen.
The minister tells us that this bill sets the legislative foundations for
disability support services.
Foundations determine what gets built next.
This legislation is not just about what happens now or today, but about
what it will allow to happen tomorrow.
As currently written, the bill does not provide a solid, safe,
and secure foundation for disability supports and allows far
too much uncertainty for what might happen tomorrow.
As noted by others, Clause 8 does not allow
disabled people to have choice and control over their lives,
including over who provides their care.
Part 1 of the bill defines family extremely broadly, and this
broad definition creates an ever-increasing circle of responsibility for
families.
The bill provides no safeguards or limits when it
comes to expectations on family care.
There is no age of majority in this bill,
and there is no age at which family is no longer considered
responsible for their disabled family member.
There is no limit in this bill on the care expected by
family and no government responsibility towards families.
Families have asked to partner with government, receive adequate payment and
recognition for their care work, and to have choice and control over the care
they provide.
This bill does not currently provide for this adequately.
Worryingly,
the bill sets the foundations for burnout, harm, and overwhelm for families.
It legislates via Clause 8 into law the very conditions that lead to
abuse and neglect.
The recent findings from the Royal Commission of Inquiry into Abuse and State Care
were clear. Disabled people must have their rights and
protections embedded into legislation.
Human rights obligations, including the UNCRPD, must
be embedded into law, and the law must guarantee disabled individuals the
right to choose their care arrangements.
Disability support services must be appropriately monitored,
regulated, and funded to ensure that disability supports are
safe and abuse-free.
These recommendations by the Royal Commission are not currently and fully
upheld in the current bill.
Foundational disability legislation must set the groundwork for
abuse-free, neglect-free lives.
It must be done in partnership with disabled people in their communities, and it
must uphold and center the rights of disabled people and families.
We know from our history that we cannot blindly trust the
government, but that our rights must be upheld in legislation.
We need this bill to uphold rights and protections, not expose
us all to potential harm.
We recommend, along with the previous submitters, starting again in
consultation with disabled people and their families. Ga-mai.

FASD is a lifelong disability. People living with it
typically have profound challenges with memory, decision-making,
impulse control,
and understanding consequences.
Many need support every day, not just as children, but throughout their
adult lives. There is no cure for FASD.
Right now, only around one in five people we represent qualify for
disability support services because of how eligibility is currently
defined. The other 80% are left without funded
support, relying almost entirely on their families.
That's already an injustice, and this bill, as drafted, risks
making it permanent and worse.
Our most serious concern is Clause 11, the power given to the minister alone
to determine who is eligible for disability support.
To explain why this matter, I just want to tell you about a typical family
situation.
Their adult son, I'll call him James, has FASD along with an
intellectual disability. His IQ has been assessed at 68, so
he does qualify for disability support services.
Qualifying on paper and actually receiving reliable support
has turned out to be two very different things.
James is 26,
can't manage money, can't hold down a job.
He cannot live safely alone.
His mother has been his carer since he was born.
She's now in her late 50s. She has her own health problems,
and she has almost no savings because she gave up her career to take up
part-time work, which allows her to care for him.
Even with James meeting the eligibility criteria, his mother has spent years
fighting the system just to get the level of support he's entitled to.
His funding has been reassessed and reduced twice.
This is what happens to a family today under a system that already involves a
measure of administrative discretion.
Clause seven, allowing the minister to redefine who qualifies
and on what terms without
parliamentary scrutiny and democracy, not requiring consultation with
the Disability Community Panel- It could be changed in time.
It's creating a lot of uncertainty for those families,

Clause eight, the family first principle, means that whenever

What is our biggest

There are several major concerns that we have with the bill.
Over-reliance on natural supports, potentially shifting

Lack of detail in the bill, with too much left to future
regulations and policy designs.
In essence, the biggest concern is that the bill creates significant risk
to access, fairness, and accountability in disability
support services due to weak design and poor consultation.
We would love to be able to answer any questions.

Ko Frian Waria toku ingoa. I am first and foremost a parent

and the founder and chairperson for NATINA,
which is the Neuroscience and Trauma-Informed Network Aotearoa.
I'm also an early intervention specialist, an
advocate in the community, and I'm a huge
promoter for systemic change that upholds inclusion, disability
rights, and well-being for our disabled children, adults, and the families.
NATINA is a charitable trust established by myself and other community
members in response to the trauma, discrimination
which we have experienced as families, members of disabled
people because of the ongoing discrimination, systemic
barriers, inadequate supports, and resources.
Our work is grounded in the belief that every person deserves to live with dignity,
belonging, and equitable opportunities to thrive.
So to answer your questions, what changes do we want to
see? We want to see
the bill ensure that disabled people over the age of
will be supported by the government of Aotearoa, New Zealand in a way that
guarantees access to the disability supports and services they
need to live a good, meaningful life and fully
realize their rights and potential.
We also ask that the bill removes the expectation that family members
will provide ongoing care and support for disabled adults.
For many families, this is simply not sustainable.
This is not because families lack love, commitment, or care for their
disabled person. Rather, many have already endured years
of hardship, discrimination, inequity, inadequate
supports, financial pressure, systemic barriers that
have impacted their own health and well-being.
Expecting families to carry lifelong caring responsibilities
without adequate supports is neither fair nor realistic.
What is our biggest concern? Our greatest concern is that the bill
creates uncertainty about the future eligibility and access
to disability supports By insisting on natural
supports first and allowing significant decisions to be made by
ministers without sufficient safeguards or meaningful
consultation with disabled people and their whānau.
In short, it increases the risk for trauma and
inequity for disabled people and their families.
What is our proposed solution? So we ask the committee to ensure that
all future decisions, amendments, considerations, implementation
of this bill are guided by trauma-informed
principles. And the principles I'm going to go by one by one.
This means upholding safety by ensuring disabled people and their whānau are
heard, valued, and supported.
It means demonstrating trustworthiness and transparency by removing
any loopholes that could allow future changes which
reduce supports or disadvantage disabled people without appropriate
accountability or consultation.
It means promoting choice, voice, and autonomy by protecting
disabled people's rights to make decisions about their own
lives and who supports them, how and where.
It means fostering collaboration and mutuality by
partnering with disabled people, whānau, disability organizations,
and Māori throughout the legislation and policy
development process,
now and in the future. It means supporting empowerment
by ensuring you give regard to all the submissions made by
disabled people and the community, and recognize their
strengths, knowledge, and lived experience that we carry.
Finally, it means embracing cultural, historical, and equity
responsiveness by recognizing the government's
obligations to Te Tiriti o Waitangi, UNCRPD, giving
regard to the outcomes from the abuse in care inquiry, addressing
inequities experienced by Māori, Pacific, and other marginalized
communities, and ensuring the disability support system is
equitable, inclusive, and accessible for all.
